question: Define the boundary of a set A in a metric space (X, d).
expectedPoints: The boundary of A, denoted ∂A, is the set of all points x in X such that every open ball centered at x intersects both A and its complement, X \ A.
Formally, x ∈ ∂A if and only if for every r > 0, B(x, r) ∩ A ≠ ∅ and B(x, r) ∩ (X \ A) ≠ ∅.
The boundary can also be defined as the closure of A minus the interior of A, ∂A = cl(A) \ int(A).

question: Prove that the boundary of a set A in a metric space is a closed set.
expectedPoints: Recall that a set is closed if it contains all of its limit points, or equivalently, if its complement is open.
An alternative approach is to show that ∂A is the intersection of two closed sets: the closure of A and the closure of its complement.
Specifically, ∂A = cl(A) ∩ cl(X \ A). Since the closure of any set is closed, and the intersection of closed sets is closed, ∂A must be closed.

question: Let Q be the set of rational numbers with the standard Euclidean metric. Determine the boundary of Q in R and justify your answer comprehensively.
expectedPoints: The boundary of Q in R is the entire real line, i.e., ∂Q = R.
Justification: For any real number x and any r > 0, the open interval (x - r, x + r) contains both rational numbers (since Q is dense in R) and irrational numbers (since R\Q is also dense in R).

Therefore, every point x in R satisfies the definition of a boundary point. This also aligns with the definition ∂Q = cl(Q) \ int(Q). Since cl(Q) = R and int(Q) = ∅ (Q has no non-empty open intervals because any interval contains irrationals), ∂Q = R \ ∅ = R.
